input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The documents mention a suite of Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) techniques and tools used for Digital Twin development, including SysML for requirements, functional, logical and product architecture modeling, and methodology families such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Component‑and‑connector architecture description languages (ADLs) cited are ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams (IBDs). Behavioral modeling approaches referenced include MontiArc Automaton, state machines in SysML v1.7, and integration with simulation environments such as MathWorks Simulink, as well as implementation in general‑purpose programming languages like C++ and Java. These are presented as the primary technical enablers for constructing Digital Twin models.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is structured around the Digital Shadow Reference Model, which defines a set of core concepts as UML classes: Purpose (the intended use), Asset (the physical entity), DataTrace (individual data points), Processing (components that execute computational logic), and Model (engineering, simulation, or data‑calculation models). A Processing component consumes one or more input DataTraces, applies a specific Model, and generates output DataTraces that become part of the digital shadow. The 'originatesFrom' association records the provenance of each DataTrace, linking it transitively to the Processing component, its input traces, and the involved Model, thereby providing fine‑grained traceability across the twin. Concretization of the reference model is achieved by mapping these abstract classes to concrete implementations such as database tables, simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) or code modules (e.g., C++/Java). This component‑based orchestration enables systematic integration of heterogeneous modeling notations and tools, supporting a coordinated flow of data through processing pipelines under the governance of the reference model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The digital shadow implements an automated one‑way data flow from the physical asset to its virtual representation. Contextual data traces such as input current, torque, and angular velocity are captured by sensors on the machine and streamed in real time via the MQTT protocol. The incoming MQTT messages are received by a software component that aggregates and abstracts the raw measurements; however, the documents do not specify the exact sampling frequencies, preprocessing algorithms, data quality‑assurance procedures, or integration mechanisms beyond the use of MQTT as the transport layer.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin development follows a Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) lifecycle in which models are produced at successive abstraction levels: (1) requirements models, (2) functional architecture models, (3) logical architecture models, and (4) product/technical (implementation) models. Representation types evolve from high‑level SysML requirement diagrams to detailed component‑and‑connector descriptions using architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams, and finally to executable artifacts in simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) or code (C++, Java). Transitions between these stages are governed by explicit conformance relations defined in the Digital Shadow reference model, preserving traceability of data traces and model elements across the lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the Digital Twin is performed within an MBSE framework that employs SysML and various architecture description languages (ADLs). The development methodology references RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT as structured approaches to handle system complexity. Models are concretized from the Digital Shadow reference model through a mapping (conformance relation) that instantiates abstract concepts with concrete classes and associations. Simulation and data‑calculation models (e.g., Simulink) are integrated with implementation code (C++, Java) to realize the twin. While specific quality‑assurance, testing, or version‑control practices are not detailed, the use of traceability links (“originates‑from” associations) provides a basis for evolution management and consistent updates.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is derived from the outputs of processing components that execute data‑calculation or simulation models on incoming contextual data traces. These components transform raw sensor or system traces into higher‑level abstract data objects that represent computed properties (e.g., aggregated performance indicators). Because every output trace is linked to its source traces and the models used, analysts can trace the provenance of an insight and assess its reliability. The resulting abstract data can be consumed by downstream analytics or visualisation tools to guide operational or engineering decisions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach relies on established modelling standards: SysML is used for requirement and internal‑block diagrams; architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc serve as standardized notations for component‑connector specifications; UML class diagrams constitute the basis of the Digital Shadow reference model and its concrete realizations. Conformance relations defined in the reference model provide a formal mechanism to ensure that concrete DT artefacts adhere to these modelling standards.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

block_1_judge.reasoning: The source documents explicitly describe the physical system studied, which is an industrial robot. The extraction incorrectly states 'Not in Document' while the text provides detailed specifications. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction claims 'Not in Document', but the source text mentions specific services provided by the digital system, such as visualization and data identification. Quotes: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard', 'enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'.

The extraction accurately and comprehensively lists the tools and methodologies found in Section II.A of the source text. It correctly includes 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE)', 'SysML', specific methodologies like 'RFLP... OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT', and architecture languages like 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc'. It also correctly cites 'MathWorks Simulink' and general-purpose languages 'C++ or Java' as part of the tooling ecosystem.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions that the system operates in 'real-time' and uses 'live measurements' and 'time series', but it does not provide specific details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic.

The text describes the creation of 'one digital shadow' for a single asset (the first axis of the robot). It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchies, or decentralized architectures.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate summary of the Digital Shadow Reference Model, correctly identifying the core UML classes (Purpose, Asset, DataTrace, Processing, Model), the data flow logic (consumption/production), and the 'originatesFrom' association for traceability. It also accurately reflects the 'concretization' process for integrating heterogeneous tools. However, the extraction includes 'database tables' as an example of concrete implementation, which is not supported by the provided source text. Supported quotes: 'Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram'; 'Processing component... consumes (uses) input data traces and produces output data'; 'originates from association, one can trace a data trace back to its generating processing component'; 'incarnates the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements'.

The text mentions 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and data being 'streamed via MQTT', which are standards and protocols. However, it focuses on the link between the specific robot (physical asset) and its digital shadow. It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins as required by the characteristic definition.

block_4_judge.reasoning:
The extraction is highly accurate, synthesizing the general definition of the digital shadow with specific technical details from the text. It correctly identifies the one-way data flow, the specific data types (input current, torque, angular velocity), and the MQTT protocol used for streaming. It also correctly notes the absence of specific sampling frequencies and detailed quality assurance procedures, which aligns with the provided text.

Quotes: 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object', 'input current and the torque and angular velocity of the gearwheel', 'streamed via MQTT'.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document' because the source text explicitly defines the system as a digital shadow which is a 'passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.'

The extracted description states 'Not in Document'. While the source mentions 'streamed via MQTT' (Section IV.A) and 'live-data linking' via 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' (Abstract), it does not provide details on network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, or redundancy mechanisms. The characteristic description requires these specific infrastructure details, which are absent from the text. Thus, stating it is 'Not in Document' regarding the *full technical connection description* is correct.

The extracted description states 'Not in Document'. The source mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' (Abstract) and the implementation of 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' (Abstract), but does not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational requirements, or deployment models. As the required details are missing, the assessment is correct.

It also correctly identifies the role of the Digital Shadow reference model and conformance relations in governing transitions and traceability.

The extraction accurately reflects the MBSE framework, specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, etc.), and the concretization process (incarnation of reference concepts) described in the source. It correctly identifies the integration of simulation models (Simulink) and code (C++, Java) and the reliance on traceability for evolution management, while accurately noting the absence of specific QA or version control details.

The description accurately reflects the Digital Shadow Reference Model's processing components described in Section II.B. nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

There is no mention of data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance with regulations, consent management, data anonymization, or data sharing agreements. The source documents primarily focus on the technical modeling of friction and the digital shadow architecture. While they mention a 'safety cell' restricting the robot's working range, the characteristic specifically asks for 'cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, risk assessment approaches, and safety-critical system considerations'.
